topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the quiet, heavy thud of a dropped stone. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt too loud in the otherwise silent apartment. The headline, the carefully crafted narrative I had poured my late nights into, was undeniably mine. But the byline? It belonged to someone else. A stranger. My hands, which had been steady while typing the final proofs, began to tremble, the paper beneath my fingertips suddenly feeling slick and treacherous. I reread the text, searching for any misplaced word, any sign of intrusion, but the words flowed, perfectly, under a borrowed identity. A coldness seeped into my chest, a paralyzing numbness that made my vision swim. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, my footsteps echoing hollowly. The sheer unfairness of it pressed down on me, making my throat tighten until a choked sound escaped.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the final draft. The realization hit me like a physical blow—a sudden drop in temperature that made my skin prickle. It wasn't a mistake; it was a complete appropriation. The name attached to the piece, the voice that had shaped those words, was not mine. A flush crept up my neck, hot and undeniable, as I leaned closer to the monitor, scrutinizing the metadata. Every line felt tainted, borrowed. I pressed my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block the sight, but the image remained burned behind my lids. A low, shaky sigh escaped my lips. I slowly pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, a small, desperate sound of protest against the injustice unfolding before me.

[story 3]

When I finally opened the published article, the world seemed to tilt. My stomach clenched into a tight knot, a painful pressure building behind my ribs. The contrast between the passion I had invested and the impersonal attribution felt like a profound betrayal. I traced the borrowed name with a fingertip, the texture of the font feeling alien and wrong. A wave of profound disorientation washed over me, leaving me feeling unsteady on my feet. I walked to the window, the city lights blurring through a sudden, unwelcome sheen of tears gathering at the corners of my eyes. Each drop felt heavy, a tangible manifestation of the deep ache settling in my core, making it hard to draw a full breath.
